insights: Only 21.8% of assets are procured in the optimal window (0–90 days before employee start), while 37.4% are purchased reactively after hire—creating service delays, onboarding friction, and urgent operational risk.

HR department exhibits the highest suboptimal procurement rate (76.9%), driven by excessive early purchases (38.5% overstock_risk), indicating systemic over-provisioning and capital inefficiency in onboarding workflows.

Server-category assets show 39.0% overstock_risk (purchased >90 days pre-start with <1-year warranty), representing both high unit cost ($8,378 mean) and severe warranty misalignment—amplifying depreciation risk and underutilization loss.

A strong inverse relationship exists between procurement lead time and warranty duration: assets purchased too early have 60.8% short warranty (<1 year), while those purchased too late have 78.1% long warranty (>2 years), revealing a compensatory but operationally harmful procurement logic.

Customer Support achieves the best alignment (26.2% optimal, 58.1% suboptimal), suggesting its hiring-to-procurement cadence and warranty planning practices can serve as a benchmark for cross-departmental policy redesign.

100% of assets have active warranties at employee start dates, confirming no immediate coverage gaps—but this masks deeper structural issues, as 62.6% of records still fall into problematic patterns (reactive_crisis or overstock_risk) that erode ROI and increase lifecycle management overhead.
summary: The analysis reveals a widespread misalignment between asset procurement timing, warranty coverage, and employee onboarding—undermining cost efficiency, service readiness, and risk control. Only 21.8% of assets follow the optimal procurement pattern, while 62.6% reflect either reactive crisis (37.4%) or overstock risk (24.2%). HR and Server-category assets are the most critical levers for intervention: HR shows the worst departmental performance (76.9% suboptimal), and Servers combine high cost with 39% overstock_risk and short warranties. The inverse relationship between early procurement and short warranties indicates a flawed compensatory strategy rather than intentional planning. Given these findings, the immediate focus should be on standardizing procurement windows by department, aligning warranty durations with expected employment tenure, and piloting Customer Support’s procurement cadence as a scalable model.
